Bearing 315 - March 1997

Orienteering News for the Pacific Northwest
March, 1997

WIOL Season Ends With Championships

The winter orienteering season ended with the WIOL Championships at Hamlin Park on February 22. The final edition of the WIOL Newsletter is included as an insert in this issue of Bearing 315, and includes complete results and a reprise of the WIOL season. Special tanks to Debbie and Linda, and all who helped them make this season another success. This promises to be an exciting spring and summer with an A-Meet, the USOF Convention, a VWC in the United States this fall, and a full schedule of events for this spring.

Coming Next

A complete listing of events for the coming months can be found on the inside back page. Here are two of the events happening next.

Seattle: Carkeek Park
Start the spring this Sunday March 16 at Carkeek Park. Starts as usual at 10-12. The map has been revised as a result of extensive slides. Carkeek Park is located along Puget Sound. Access is off of 3rd Ave. NW at NW 110th St. Meet Director is Bill Cusworth. Courses by Harvey Friedman.

Tri- Cities: Horn Rapids
If you're near the Tri-Cities area on March 16, and don't fell like coming to Carkeek Park., start your spring orienteering at Horn Rapids, near Richland. Starts are 1-3 pm. Meet Director is Frank Skorina.

ECOCHALLENGE - Meet a Challenger

Did you watch the EcoChallenge up in British Columbia? If you did, you will want to mark Saturday, March 29 (it's the day before Easter). Cascade's Alex Hall was on the team that finished seventh overall! He will have maps, slides, and stories galore. Then it's potluck time at the Reddicks', about 5 or 6.
Time: Start at 3 pm, end - whenever. Place: Bob Reddick's in Kenmore: 18232 58th Ave. NE. Phone 487-0099

PIG WAR IX

Yes, it's Pig War time again. Pig War IX will be brought to you this year by the Victorienteers. As a change of pace, competition for the trophy this year will be a Relay. There will be Three to a Team. The Relay will be at Beaver Lake Park on Sunday, 23 March, and will be the second day of a two day meet. The first day will be a standard B meet at Thetis Lake on Saturday.

Following the meet on Saturday, there will be a social at 6:30 (location at meet). Let's try for a good turnout. We have to keep the trophy in Eileen's closet.
[Image: An ink drawing of a wild pig or boar standing sideways]

Then, there is the rumour that the rules have been changed, and the winner this year gets to determine who keeps the trophy.

Huskies Host Training at Magnuson Park

On Saturday, March 29, the Husky Orienteering & Running Club will host a training day at Magnuson Park. There will be training on logistical tasks of hosting orienteering meets, such as registration, start, and finish timing; a course setting workshop; and orienteering skills training courses.
Register from 9:30; the activities will start at 10 a.m.
Cascade Orienteering Club sustaining members may participate for free. The usual meet fees apply for others, but donations to support the Husky Intercollegiate team travelling to Missouri are welcome. Contact Eric Bone 206-634-2279 with any questions.

Order a Washington State O' Suit

If you would like to order a classic Washington State O' suit (burgundy, teal, and black), please call Debbie Newell (488-7471) by April 1. We need a minimum of 8 orders to get a batch, so call right away if you want one. Approximate cost is $45 per suit.

Field Notes

By Rick Hood

WIOL Success
130 Students participated in the Intererscholastic Orienteering League this season. Congratulations to each of them and all of Debbie Newell's WIOL staff that made the season possible.

COC Mapping
The club will be updating the Trollhaugen map shortly and doing two major new map in the Cle Elum area (Teanaway Forks and Lick Creek). If you would like to be considered for anypart of either effort, please contact club president Bill Cusworth by March 20th for the Trollhaugen project or April 20th for the new map project. (Bill can be reached at 789-1346.)

Trail Work
COC will be hosting some trail work project in St. Edward State Park during June and July and would love your help for a few hours or a few days. Please contact Rick Hood at 367-2606 if you are willing to help out.

Bullfrog Area
Kent Verbeck has spokn to the new owners of the Bullfrog map area (Trend West Resorts) and is pleased to report that Sammamish is likely to be able to have access to one, if not both, map area for the foreseeable future.

Rogaine
Congratulations to Rick Breseman and Kean Williams who took third place, out of 15 teams, in the 12 hour division at the Rogaine Championships in Arizona this past weekend. (Howard Halter and partner also did great taking fourth overall in the 12 hours class.) We don't know how the master's division team of Bob Reddick and KenLew did, as Rick flew home early just to get us this new flash, but when Rick saw them out on the course he said Bob and Ken were still going strong.

Meet Reports

Hamlin Park
Typically, mid February is likely to be cold, rainy and drear, but the WIOL Championship was a warm, sunny day, and made a fitting climax to a fine WIOL season. As befits a championship, the courses were all pre-printed, all vetted, and with a standard three-stage start. Special thanks to pat Reddick for the cookies, and to Bill Cusworth for being there so early to vet the courses.

Fire Mountain - Washington State Intercollegiates
By contrast, Fire Mountain was a day to test one's determination to orienteer. Typical Fire Mountain in the winter—temperature in the mid 30s, wind 25 knots, and rain blowing slightly horizontal, and snow on the hillside. But Paul's courses were great, no complaints about misplaced controls, and we saw a new area of the map. One disappointment was a small showing of college students. Not much of a surprise, with Eric Bone, UW taking first, and Tyler Fredericksen from Western Washington taking second.

NORTH AMERICAN ROGAINING CHAMPS

Arizona Rogaine 1997
by Bob Reddick

Quoting the event information by Wilkey Richardson: "The weather in Arizona around the first of March is usually sunny and mild. It is possible, however...." Ken Lew and I drove into the Empire Ranch meet area southeast of Tucson at about 11:30 PM on Friday night (Feb 28th) in a blinding snowstorm.

Almost couldn't find the ranch road, in spite of detailed directions. Setting up a tent in the snow on a cow-pasture littered with snow-covered unknowns at midnight, then trying to sleep in sub-freezing temperatures before the 10 AM start was somewhat challenging. The North American Championships for Rogaining, plus 8 and 12-hour events was well attended by about 52 teams, with competitors from Western and Eastern Canada, and throughout the U.S. Many ultra-marathon types, an Eco-Challenge team in training, and those us of just there for fun had a great time!

By 11 AM on Saturday the weather had warmed, the sun came out, and the rest of the event was just about perfect for weather - given the 4600+ ft. elevation and the dry desert air. Teams scattered in all directions from the base camp (Hash House) at the start time - going for scores at the 65 controls scattered over nearly 200 square KM of former ranch country (now BLM land) and the Coronado National Forest. Controls were all well-sited, the map was excellent, and the navigation was generally easy. Lots and lots of barbed-wire fences made nice handrails, but slowed progress for those not able to leap over four feet with a pack on! Hostile plants were common, and sharp spines, some with hooks, awaited the unwary. Many birds, some deer, and herds of javelina (a wild boar), jackrabbits, and one black coyote were spotted. The comet was seen by those up at 5 AM on Sunday.

Perhaps the finest new map ever produced for a rogaine was put together by Roger Sperline. It was a 1:30,000 full color, well-field checked one issued in six 8 1/2 by 11 inch sections, with an overall 1:75,000 black and white one for planning overall routes. The Tucson Orienteering Club's sixth rogaine had marvelous organization, great food, and a very friendly atmosphere. All the right things to send the competitors home happy. An emergency food run was even made Saturday night to Tucson to replace the hot items which were consumed in astonishing quantities by cold and hungry rogainers. No real injuries, no lost teams, and fast and efficient results and awards over by 11:15 AM Sunday completed the perfect event.

Oh! Who won? Well, in my admittedly biased view, every competitor did! For a complete report of the results and comments from the competitors, see the next issue of Orienteering/North America. Some preliminary results for Northwest orienteers: Rick Breseman, Kean Williams (3rd, 12 h), Dave Petty, Knut Olson (8th , 8 hr), Howard Halter, Steve Stevens (4th M21, 12 hr), Mark Hartinger, Gene Trahern (4th M21, 24 hr), John Britton, Vladimir Gustiatnikov (3rd M21, 24 hr), Les and Beth Stark (6th, Mix 8 hr), Bob Reddick, Ken Lew (8th M45, 24 hr) .

GLITF,
Bob

Your Chance to Help - Volunteers Needed

Cascade Orienteering Club will be holding A-meets in August of both this year and next, and we need the help of club volunteers to make themsuccessful. If the U.S. Orienteering Federation approves our plan, these meets will have an individual classic distance race on Saturday and a four-person relay on Sunday, and the 1998 event (Aug. 8-9) will be both the U.S. classic and relay championships. The 1997 event (Aug. 23-24) is intended as a practice for the 1998 U.S. Champs bid meet, so we would like for the key staff for the two evnets to be the same. The technical team, which I (Eric Bone) head, is responsible for preparing the courses; this covers discussing course setting principles, preliminary design, critiquing, test running, marker placement, vetting, preparing course setter's notes, and more— anything between the start and finish lines.

Here are positions for which we need volunteers:
Course designers (both on paper and in the field)
Classic distance: White, Yellow, Orange, Brown, Green, Red and Blue
Advanced skill and experience is desirable for brown and up course(s). At least intermediate skill and experience is desirable for the Orange course designer, and some experience is desirable for the White and Yellow course designer(s).
Relay: Leg 1 (Orange), Leg 2 (Yellow), Leg 3 (Green), Leg 4(Red)
Don't worry if you don't know what the course names mean or if you don't know much about course design; Same qualifications for relay design as above.
Course consultants (review, comment on, and suggest changes to draft courses by course designers. Field visits are helpful, but not mandatory.)
Test runners (run draft course(s) to test for appropriate length and give general feedback. This does not require a long-term commitment.)
Control setters (place stands and markers according to course setters' flagging. This could be done by other members of the technical team f they are willing.)
Vetter (check all control locations to verify map accuracy and correct placement of marker or flagging. The vetter could have helpers, but all would need to be competent and experienced orienteers.)
Pre-runners (run the courses early on the morning of the event to make sure controls are set-up correctly and in good condition and report any problems to the technical team.)

If any of these activities sound interesting to you and you'd like more information, you're welcome to contact me. If you want to help out in another way, there are many other opportunities; just ask. And remember, putting on A-meets is a team effort; we need volunteers to pull this off.
Thanks!
Eric Bone, Technical Coordinator. 206-634-2279 ebone@u.washington.edu

AN INVITATION TO TWO EVENTS IN EASTERN WASHINGTON THIS SPRING

Enclosed in this issue is an entry form for the seventh Inland Northwest Cup and the first Inland Northwest Relay at Riverside State Park in Spokane May 31 - June 1. (The closure of the footbridge across the Spokane River at the Bowl and Pitcher due to safety considerations has necessitated the choice of a different parking area for the relay than has been used on Sunday morning in past years. Consult the entry form for directions.)

For those who would like to see some new terrain in eastern Washington, EWOC is also offering a three-hour Score O at Fishtrap Lake on April 27, near Sprague, Washington. In the following article, John Harbuck describes how the area was selected and his experience designing the first Score O at Fishtrap Lake last September. You may participate in the Score O as individuals or in a group. Fees are $4 per map for members of an orienteering club; there will also be a novice course for $3. Registration will begin at 9:00 a.m., and there will be a mass start at 10:00.

To get to the event site, use Exit 254 on I-90 and go south to entrance to the BLM land. Because there are no restrooms at the site, you are advised to make a rest stop before getting off I-90.

For those looking for a Saturday night campsite, Four Seasons Campground, (509) 257-2332, offers RV hookups at $17 per night, tent sites (with free showers but no water at the tent site) for $13 per night, and cabins for four persons for $55 per night. To get to the camp-ground take the Sprague exit off I-90 and follow the campground signs.

THE FISHTRAP LAKE SCORE O

EWOC's first score meet at Fishtrap Lake last September 28 came about in a rather roundabout way. While laboring up a hill at Ros-Elum Ridge a couple of summers ago, I encountered Anne York, an orienteer from the west side. She was orienteering with bin-oculars and bird book as well as map and compass. On learning I was from EWOC, she asked whether I had ever considered Fishtrap. Since I had been orienteering for less than a year at the time, of course I hadn't ... but the seed was planted. Somewhat later, Marshall Nelson complained that all our score meets were too short; at his relaxed pace, he didn't get to visit enough controls. Add to the above, someone's stating that they thought the future of orienteering lay in ROGAINEs, where one can just use USGS topo maps, and all the pieces were there.

Last December, with brand new topo map in hand, I visited Fishtrap Lake for the first time and was greatly impressed. I found out that what had been for years a working ranch had been transferred a few years ago to the BLM. They are managing it for grazing, wildlife, and recreation; hence, public access is guaran-teed. The 8 sq.mi. (20 sq.km) of terrain provide excellent orienteering. The western part is mostly open rolling grasslands, with some basalt rock features. As one approaches the lake to the east, one enters an open ponderosa pine forest in wonderful "channeled scablands" topography — abrupt basalt buttes and a maze of little canyons, with a couple dozen sparkling pothole lakes. Ducks, hawks, osprey, and owls abound; and Isaw snipe plus any numbe of LGBs (little gray birds) for which I needed binoculars and didn't have them. The fourteen people who turned out all seemed to have a good time.So . . . next April will see a THREE hour score meet at Fishtrap! See you then.

The Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League Newsletter

1996-97 Season - Final Issue

Running, Reading, & Writing

Even though the WIOL season is over, there are many super orienteering options both near town and within a few hours' drive this spring, summer, and fall. (If you are 18 or under, many of these events are priced at just three dollars apiece.) To find out more about these, just look at this and future issues of the Bearing 315 or call the Orienteering Hotline at 783-FunO.

And if you want to read a good book on orienteering techniques, consider Orienteering Skill and Strategies, $22.50, available from A&E Orienteering at (314) 872-3185. This or other orienteering books would make an excellent addition to a team or school library. (A&E also has a very complete O catalog, free for the asking.)

Lastly, thanks to everyone who filled out a WIOL survey; your comments are really important. Currently, each and every one is being reviewed by Debbie Newell, this year's Director. Debbie will pass on a summary and the cards to next year's WIOL Director such that your suggestions are given full weight. Again, thank you for your input!!!

COC Donates Memberships

by Debbie Newell, WIOL Director

Cascade Orienteering Club has donated free club memberships to the top three male and female high school finishers for the 1997 WIOL season. Sean Bemis (Bremerton), Justin Wright (Thomas Jefferson), Jason Dempsey (Bethel), Tara Matthews (Thomas Jefferson), Rosalyn Pham (Bremerton), and Ashley Matthews (Thomas Jefferson) will receive 1-year memberships in the Cascade Orienteering Club. These memberships include a subscription to COC's newsletter, Bearing 315.

Tara and Sean will also receive 1-year student memberships in the US Orienteering Federation (donated by the Washington State Orienteering Association), which includes a subscription to O/NA, the orienteering magazine of North America. Congratulations to all for their fine performances.

[COC will also donate an orienteering book to each of the season's top three schools. – ed.]

The Eastern Washington Orienteering Club presents

The Seventh Inland Northwest Orienteering Cup and Relay

B Meet
May 31 - June 1, 1997
Riverside State Park, Spokane

Terrain
Open, runnable pine forest

Map
Produced in 1989 for the Washington Centennial Games; 1:15,000 scale 5-color International Orienteering Federation-standard map. Field-checked by Al Smith and Ulf Köster, Cartography by Gary McCausland.

Courses
Five courses on Saturday, designed by Bill Borrie. Sunday will be a three-person team relay. More information on the Sunday relay will be available at the event registration on Saturday.

Categories
The categories offered on Saturday are listed on the back of this page. However, some categories may be combined if fewer than five people enter them.

Awards
All participants in competitive categories (regardless of residence) are eligible for Inland Northwest Cup awards to be presented to the top three finishers in each category on Saturday. Inland Northwest Cup trophies will be awarded to the top woman on Course 4 and the top man on Course 5. Awards for the Saturday courses will be presented at the dinner Saturday evening. Awards to the winning teams in the relay will be presented Sunday morning.

Start Times
Saturday - 1:00 to 3:00 P.M. Park at ORV area.

Training Notes: Pace Counting

The best way to learn about pacing is to do it yourself. There are all sorts of ways to do this. What I recommend is measuring out 50 meters in a flat area. Walk that distance numerous times and count the number of times your left OR right foot hit the ground. Average that number and multiply it by 2. That gives you the number of paces you need for 100m. You can divide or multiply that number as needed to determine very rough distances at all your orienteering meets.

Next, try the same thing for uphills and downhills. If you take twice the number of paces on an uphill just remember to double the pace count for steep uphills.

Most important of all, when you begin pacing, look at a distant object in the direction you are heading and then go directly for that object. If something is in your way, take some side steps and then continue pacing in parallel to your original direction. Once you are past the object, side step back to the original path and continue pacing.

Pacing is only a rough measure so never expect it to be exact. Very often I need to go say 300m N to find something on the map. When I reach 300m I quickly scan for the object to confirm my location. I then go to the object and pace in another direction say 400m to the control. I always pace to a general area and when there I look for something to place myself exactly on a mapped feature (a reentrant, a huge rock, a creek bend, etc.). For each paced route, there is a percentage error. If you don't find something there to place yourself on the map, then the next paced route will add to the error. After two or three pacings without locating on a feature, you could find yourself way out of the area you need to be in.

Tony Pinkham BAOC

block_index.text: Orienteering News for the Pacific Northwest March, 1997

The winter orienteering season ended with the WIOL Championships at Hamlin Park on February 22. The final edition of the WIOL Newsletter is included as an insert in this issue of Bearing 315, and includes complete results and a reprise of the WIOL season. Special tanks to Debbie and Linda, and all who helped them make this season another success. This promises to be an exciting spring and summer with an A-Meet, the USOF Convention, a VWC in the United States this fall, and a full schedule of events for this spring.
A complete listing of events for the coming months can be found on the inside back page. Here are two of the events happening next.

Seattle: Carkeek Park Start the spring this Sunday March 16 at Carkeek Park. Starts as usual at 10-12. The map has been revised as a result of extensive slides. Carkeek Park is located along Puget Sound. Access is off of 3rd Ave. NW at NW 110th St. Meet Director is Bill Cusworth. Courses by Harvey Friedman.

Tri- Cities: Horn Rapids If you're near the Tri-Cities area on March 16, and don't fell like coming to Carkeek Park., start your spring orienteering at Horn Rapids, near Richland. Starts are 1-3 pm. Meet Director is Frank Skorina.

Did you watch the EcoChallenge up in British Columbia? If you did, you will want to mark Saturday, March 29 (it's the day before Easter). Cascade's Alex Hall was on the team that finished seventh overall! He will have maps, slides, and stories galore. Then it's potluck time at the Reddicks', about 5 or 6. Time: Start at 3 pm, end - whenever. Place: Bob Reddick's in Kenmore: 18232 58th Ave. NE. Phone 487-0099

> Inside this Issue: > p. 2 Field Notes > p. 2 Rogaine Report > p. 3 Inland Cup > p. 3 Volunteers Needed > p. 4 Meet Results > p. 6 Winter O' Series Final Standings > > Copyright 1997, Cascade Orienteering Club. Permission is given to reproduce for the promotion of orienteering. Bearing 315 is published six times each year by Cascade Orienteering Club.

Yes, it's Pig War time again. Pig War IX will be brought to you this year by the Victorienteers. As a change of pace, competition for the trophy this year will be a Relay. There will be Three to a Team. The Relay will be at Beaver Lake Park on Sunday, 23 March, and will be the second day of a two day meet. The first day will be a standard B meet at Thetis Lake on Saturday.

Following the meet on Saturday, there will be a social at 6:30 (location at meet). Let's try for a good turnout. We have to keep the trophy in Eileen's closet. [Image: An ink drawing of a wild pig or boar standing sideways]
Then, there is the rumour that the rules have been changed, and the winner this year gets to determine who keeps the trophy.
B-Meet: 1:00-2:30 Starts. Map is 1:15000. 5 m contours Meet Director: Gail MacDonald Courses: Mike Teachman
Relay 9:00 Late Resignation 10:00 Start Map is 1:10,000 5 m contour Meet Director: Carl Coger Courses: Steve Chatwin
Huskies Host Training at Magnuson Park

On Saturday, March 29, the Husky Orienteering & Running Club will host a training day at Magnuson Park. There will be training on logistical tasks of hosting orienteering meets, such as registration, start, and finish timing; a course setting workshop; and orienteering skills training courses. Register from 9:30; the activities will start at 10 a.m. Cascade Orienteering Club sustaining members may participate for free. The usual meet fees apply for others, but donations to support the Husky Intercollegiate team travelling to Missouri are welcome. Contact Eric Bone 206-634-2279 with any questions.
Order a Washington State O' Suit

If you would like to order a classic Washington State O' suit (burgundy, teal, and black), please call Debbie Newell (488-7471) by April 1. We need a minimum of 8 orders to get a batch, so call right away if you want one. Approximate cost is $45 per suit.
WIOL Success 130 Students participated in the Intererscholastic Orienteering League this season. Congratulations to each of them and all of Debbie Newell's WIOL staff that made the season possible.

COC Mapping The club will be updating the Trollhaugen map shortly and doing two major new map in the Cle Elum area (Teanaway Forks and Lick Creek). If you would like to be considered for anypart of either effort, please contact club president Bill Cusworth by March 20th for the Trollhaugen project or April 20th for the new map project. (Bill can be reached at 789-1346.)

Trail Work COC will be hosting some trail work project in St. Edward State Park during June and July and would love your help for a few hours or a few days. Please contact Rick Hood at 367-2606 if you are willing to help out.

Bullfrog Area Kent Verbeck has spokn to the new owners of the Bullfrog map area (Trend West Resorts) and is pleased to report that Sammamish is likely to be able to have access to one, if not both, map area for the foreseeable future.

Rogaine Congratulations to Rick Breseman and Kean Williams who took third place, out of 15 teams, in the 12 hour division at the Rogaine Championships in Arizona this past weekend. (Howard Halter and partner also did great taking fourth overall in the 12 hours class.) We don't know how the master's division team of Bob Reddick and KenLew did, as Rick flew home early just to get us this new flash, but when Rick saw them out on the course he said Bob and Ken were still going strong.

Hamlin Park Typically, mid February is likely to be cold, rainy and drear, but the WIOL Championship was a warm, sunny day, and made a fitting climax to a fine WIOL season. As befits a championship, the courses were all pre-printed, all vetted, and with a standard three-stage start. Special thanks to pat Reddick for the cookies, and to Bill Cusworth for being there so early to vet the courses.

Fire Mountain - Washington State Intercollegiates By contrast, Fire Mountain was a day to test one's determination to orienteer. Typical Fire Mountain in the winter—temperature in the mid 30s, wind 25 knots, and rain blowing slightly horizontal, and snow on the hillside. But Paul's courses were great, no complaints about misplaced controls, and we saw a new area of the map. One disappointment was a small showing of college students. Not much of a surprise, with Eric Bone, UW taking first, and Tyler Fredericksen from Western Washington taking second.

Quoting the event information by Wilkey Richardson: "The weather in Arizona around the first of March is usually sunny and mild. It is possible, however...." Ken Lew and I drove into the Empire Ranch meet area southeast of Tucson at about 11:30 PM on Friday night (Feb 28th) in a blinding snowstorm.

Almost couldn't find the ranch road, in spite of detailed directions. Setting up a tent in the snow on a cow-pasture littered with snow-covered unknowns at midnight, then trying to sleep in sub-freezing temperatures before the 10 AM start was somewhat challenging. The North American Championships for Rogaining, plus 8 and 12-hour events was well attended by about 52 teams, with competitors from Western and Eastern Canada, and throughout the U.S. Many ultra-marathon types, an Eco-Challenge team in training, and those us of just there for fun had a great time!

By 11 AM on Saturday the weather had warmed, the sun came out, and the rest of the event was just about perfect for weather - given the 4600+ ft. elevation and the dry desert air. Teams scattered in all directions from the base camp (Hash House) at the start time - going for scores at the 65 controls scattered over nearly 200 square KM of former ranch country (now BLM land) and the Coronado National Forest. Controls were all well-sited, the map was excellent, and the navigation was generally easy. Lots and lots of barbed-wire fences made nice handrails, but slowed progress for those not able to leap over four feet with a pack on! Hostile plants were common, and sharp spines, some with hooks, awaited the unwary. Many birds, some deer, and herds of javelina (a wild boar), jackrabbits, and one black coyote were spotted. The comet was seen by those up at 5 AM on Sunday.

Perhaps the finest new map ever produced for a rogaine was put together by Roger Sperline. It was a 1:30,000 full color, well-field checked one issued in six 8 1/2 by 11 inch sections, with an overall 1:75,000 black and white one for planning overall routes. The Tucson Orienteering Club's sixth rogaine had marvelous organization, great food, and a very friendly atmosphere. All the right things to send the competitors home happy. An emergency food run was even made Saturday night to Tucson to replace the hot items which were consumed in astonishing quantities by cold and hungry rogainers. No real injuries, no lost teams, and fast and efficient results and awards over by 11:15 AM Sunday completed the perfect event.

Oh! Who won? Well, in my admittedly biased view, every competitor did! For a complete report of the results and comments from the competitors, see the next issue of Orienteering/North America. Some preliminary results for Northwest orienteers: Rick Breseman, Kean Williams (3rd, 12 h), Dave Petty, Knut Olson (8th , 8 hr), Howard Halter, Steve Stevens (4th M21, 12 hr), Mark Hartinger, Gene Trahern (4th M21, 24 hr), John Britton, Vladimir Gustiatnikov (3rd M21, 24 hr), Les and Beth Stark (6th, Mix 8 hr), Bob Reddick, Ken Lew (8th M45, 24 hr) .
Your Chance to Help - Volunteers Needed

Cascade Orienteering Club will be holding A-meets in August of both this year and next, and we need the help of club volunteers to make themsuccessful. If the U.S. Orienteering Federation approves our plan, these meets will have an individual classic distance race on Saturday and a four-person relay on Sunday, and the 1998 event (Aug. 8-9) will be both the U.S. classic and relay championships. The 1997 event (Aug. 23-24) is intended as a practice for the 1998 U.S. Champs bid meet, so we would like for the key staff for the two evnets to be the same. The technical team, which I (Eric Bone) head, is responsible for preparing the courses; this covers discussing course setting principles, preliminary design, critiquing, test running, marker placement, vetting, preparing course setter's notes, and more— anything between the start and finish lines.

Here are positions for which we need volunteers: Course designers (both on paper and in the field) Classic distance: White, Yellow, Orange, Brown, Green, Red and Blue Advanced skill and experience is desirable for brown and up course(s). At least intermediate skill and experience is desirable for the Orange course designer, and some experience is desirable for the White and Yellow course designer(s). Relay: Leg 1 (Orange), Leg 2 (Yellow), Leg 3 (Green), Leg 4(Red) Don't worry if you don't know what the course names mean or if you don't know much about course design; Same qualifications for relay design as above. Course consultants (review, comment on, and suggest changes to draft courses by course designers. Field visits are helpful, but not mandatory.) Test runners (run draft course(s) to test for appropriate length and give general feedback. This does not require a long-term commitment.) Control setters (place stands and markers according to course setters' flagging. This could be done by other members of the technical team f they are willing.) Vetter (check all control locations to verify map accuracy and correct placement of marker or flagging. The vetter could have helpers, but all would need to be competent and experienced orienteers.) Pre-runners (run the courses early on the morning of the event to make sure controls are set-up correctly and in good condition and report any problems to the technical team.)

If any of these activities sound interesting to you and you'd like more information, you're welcome to contact me. If you want to help out in another way, there are many other opportunities; just ask. And remember, putting on A-meets is a team effort; we need volunteers to pull this off. Thanks! You may participate in the Score O as individuals or in a group. Fees are $4 per map for members of an orienteering club; there will also be a novice course for $3. EWOC's first score meet at Fishtrap Lake last September 28 came about in a rather roundabout way. While laboring up a hill at Ros-Elum Ridge a couple of summers ago, I encountered Anne York, an orienteer from the west side. She was orienteering with bin-oculars and bird book as well as map and compass. On learning I was from EWOC, she asked whether I had ever considered Fishtrap. Since I had been orienteering for less than a year at the time, of course I hadn't ... but the seed was planted. Somewhat later, Marshall Nelson complained that all our score meets were too short; at his relaxed pace, he didn't get to visit enough controls. Add to the above, someone's stating that they thought the future of orienteering lay in ROGAINEs, where one can just use USGS topo maps, and all the pieces were there.

Last December, with brand new topo map in hand, I visited Fishtrap Lake for the first time and was greatly impressed. I found out that what had been for years a working ranch had been transferred a few years ago to the BLM. They are managing it for grazing, wildlife, and recreation; hence, public access is guaran-teed. The 8 sq.mi. (20 sq.km) of terrain provide excellent orienteering. The western part is mostly open rolling grasslands, with some basalt rock features. As one approaches the lake to the east, one enters an open ponderosa pine forest in wonderful "channeled scablands" topography — abrupt basalt buttes and a maze of little canyons, with a couple dozen sparkling pothole lakes. Ducks, hawks, osprey, and owls abound; and Isaw snipe plus any numbe of LGBs (little gray birds) for which I needed binoculars and didn't have them. The fourteen people who turned out all seemed to have a good time.So . . . next April will see a THREE hour score meet at Fishtrap! See you then.

The best way to learn about pacing is to do it yourself. There are all sorts of ways to do this. What I recommend is measuring out 50 meters in a flat area. Walk that distance numerous times and count the number of times your left OR right foot hit the ground. Average that number and multiply it by 2. That gives you the number of paces you need for 100m. You can divide or multiply that number as needed to determine very rough distances at all your orienteering meets.
Next, try the same thing for uphills and downhills. If you take twice the number of paces on an uphill just remember to double the pace count for steep uphills.

Most important of all, when you begin pacing, look at a distant object in the direction you are heading and then go directly for that object. If something is in your way, take some side steps and then continue pacing in parallel to your original direction. Once you are past the object, side step back to the original path and continue pacing.

Pacing is only a rough measure so never expect it to be exact. Very often I need to go say 300m N to find something on the map. When I reach 300m I quickly scan for the object to confirm my location. I then go to the object and pace in another direction say 400m to the control. I always pace to a general area and when there I look for something to place myself exactly on a mapped feature (a reentrant, a huge rock, a creek bend, etc.). For each paced route, there is a percentage error. If you don't find something there to place yourself on the map, then the next paced route will add to the error. After two or three pacings without locating on a feature, you could find yourself way out of the area you need to be in.
